title: Baked ziti
subtitle: Tube pasta baked with tomato sauce, ricotta, and a top of melted mozzarella.
excerpt: An Italian-American baked pasta dish. Ziti tubes are tossed with a long-cooked tomato sauce and spoonfuls of seasoned ricotta, then baked under a heavy cap of grated mozzarella. The point is the contrast between the soft, sauced pasta below and the brown bubbled cheese on top.
sourceNotes: Baked ziti (ziti al forno) is part of the southern-Italian and Italian-American baked-pasta family that also includes pasta al forno, baked rigatoni, and lasagna. The American household version emerged in the early twentieth century as Italian immigrants adapted home cooking to American ingredients and oven sizes. It belongs to a family of dishes (Sicilian pasta al forno, Neapolitan timballo) that bake pasta with sauce and cheese into a single sliceable piece. Mrs Beeton (Book of Household Management, 1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) describes a baked macaroni cheese on closely related lines.

recipe.freezeNotes: Assembled but unbaked, the dish freezes well for up to two months. Wrap tightly, defrost overnight in the fridge, then bake from cold, adding ten minutes. Baked-and-cooled portions reheat acceptably but the top loses its crust.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly into two trays. One serves four to six; the second goes in the freezer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sauce and ricotta mixture can be made a day ahead. Pasta can be cooked and tossed with sauce up to four hours before baking; cover and refrigerate, then bake straight from the fridge, adding five minutes.

content.text: Tube pasta, tomato sauce, ricotta, mozzarella. Cook the pasta short of the packet time so it has somewhere to go in the oven. Mix it through the sauce off the heat, not at the boil; pasta that boils in sauce keeps cooking and the dish bakes mushy. Spoon dollops of seasoned ricotta through the pasta rather than mixing them in, so the bake stays striped: pasta on the outside, ricotta in pockets inside, melted mozzarella across the top.

Ziti is the right shape, but penne, rigatoni, or any short tube works. Long pasta does not bake well; the strands break and clump. Block mozzarella, grated, gives the brown top; wet fresh mozzarella does not.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} chopped onion and a pinch of salt. Cook for eight minutes, until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of chopped garlic and the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée; cook for one minute more, stirring. Pour in the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tomatoes and add the {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Bring to a simmer, season with salt and pepper, and cook uncovered for twenty-five minutes, until the sauce has thickened. Stir through the {{basil}} of basil.

In a medium bowl, beat the {{ricotta}} of ricotta with the {{eggs}} egg, the {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley, a generous pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper. Stir in about 30 g of the grated parmesan from the topping. The mixture should be thick and just spoonable.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Bring a stockpot of well-salted water to a rolling boil. Add the {{pasta-dried}} of pasta and cook for three minutes less than the packet time. Drain, reserving a cup of pasta water. Tip the pasta back into the empty pot, off the heat. Pour over three-quarters of the sauce. Stir to coat. Add half of the {{mozzarella}} of grated mozzarella and stir again. The pasta should be glossy and well coated; loosen with a splash of pasta water if needed.

Spread half the pasta into a deep roasting pan. Dot half the ricotta mixture in spoonfuls across the pasta. Repeat with the remaining pasta and ricotta. Spoon the rest of the tomato sauce over the top, then scatter the remaining mozzarella and the {{parmesan}} of grated parmesan across the surface. Bake at 200°C for twenty-five to thirty minutes, until the top is deeply browned and the sauce bubbles at the edges. Rest for ten minutes before serving; the bake holds its shape on the spoon after the rest.

items.cause: Pasta cooked too long before going in the oven.
items.fix: Drain three minutes short of the packet time. The pasta keeps cooking in the oven; under-cook the boil.

items.symptom: Dish is dry under the cheese top
items.cause: Not enough sauce, or pasta absorbed it before baking.
items.fix: Loosen the pasta with a generous splash of reserved pasta water before assembling. Keep enough sauce for the layer on top.

items.symptom: Top is pale rather than brown
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or wet mozzarella used.
items.fix: Use block mozzarella, not the wet fresh sort. Push the temperature to 210°C for the last five minutes if needed. The browning is from the cheese, not the sauce.
Ricotta needs salt to taste of anything. Season the mixture aggressively; the pasta and sauce balance it.

items.symptom: Edges burnt before the middle was hot
items.cause: Roasting pan too shallow, or oven shelf too high.
items.fix: Use a deep pan and bake on the middle shelf. Cover with foil for the first fifteen minutes if the top is browning before the centre has warmed.

content.text: Brown 250 g of crumbled pork sausage with the onion for a heartier bake. A handful of chopped olives or capers turns it more southern Italian. For a vegetarian deepening, fold in a layer of cooked spinach or sautéed mushrooms between pasta and ricotta. The American Sunday-night version often adds a layer of provolone or scamorza for an extra cheese pull.

Baked ziti is the bring-to-a-gathering dish of Italian-American kitchens. It is the dish that goes in the oven before a wake, that arrives at a new neighbour's house, that fills a deep tray for Sunday supper. Sicilian and southern-Italian pasta al forno follows the same shape: short pasta, sauce, cheese, the oven. The difference is in the proportions. The American version is heavier on the cheese and ricotta; the Italian version pulls towards the pasta.
